    remarks => UTF-8 string (2406) "ISSUE: The Petitioner is a reputed funeral undertaker and imported a Hearse ...
    $value[36]['remarks']
    ISSUE:
The Petitioner is a reputed funeral undertaker and imported a Hearse Vehicle for its VIP services somewhere around in 2017. Accordingly, the Petitioner has settled the payable duty to the Customs upon an Assessment Notice on which the rate of the excise duty has been recognized as the rate in place at the time of opening the LC. After the payment of the excise duty, the Declaration Unit of Sri Lanka Customs has allegedly prevented releasing the import documents of the vehicle on account of the budget revision. Anyhow, the Petitioner was allowed to release the vehicle upon a bank guarantee valued for the short levy. Later, the Petitioner had got to know that there was a Gazette Extraordinary in operation at the time of clearing the said vehicle and thereby the vehicle in question had attracted higher excise duty than paid. Further, it was revealed to him that the Customs Asycuda system had not been properly updated in line with the duty hike. In the meantime, the Ministry of Finance has missed the category of Hearse in its subsequent Gazette was to give reliefs. Considering all these impediments, the Petitioner moved to a writ application seeking relief from the Court of Appeal to get released the import documents pertaining to the vehicle and the same be registered in the Department of Mortar Vehicle.

JUDGEMENT:
**Respondents heavily rely on provisions of Section 3 of the Protection of Government Revenue (Special Provisions) Act No. 01 of 2006, where it set outs the date of Importation or exportation shall be the date of delivery to the DGC of the bill of entry on which tax is levied.
**If this position was correct, the subsequent relief Gazette could not be enforced in view of Section 3 of the PGR Act. 
**Assertions on the legitimate expectation of the importer
**Assertion on the inadvertently update of the System by Sri Lanka Customs that the system has not been modified to collect taxes based on Engine Capacity for Hearses
**Assertion on the non-inclusion of the Hearse into the relief Gazette published subsequently
**Hence, the Petitioner’s rights have been affected due to the conduct of the Customs and the Department of Fiscal Policy
**Once the legitimacy of the expectation is established, the court will have the task of weighing the requirements of fairness against any overriding interest relied upon for the changing policy.

HELD:
Reliefs Granted

    ⇄url => string (30) "1667385187CA-WRIT-454-2019.pdf"

    ⇄⧉
    remarks => string (1997) "ISSUE: Petitioner had imported a vehicle and after paying Vehicle Luxury Tax...
    $value[38]['remarks']
    ISSUE:
Petitioner had imported a vehicle and after paying Vehicle Luxury Tax he cleared the same from the Customs.. At the time of importation of the said vehicle, the Minister of Finance has only signed the Regulation pertaining to the Vehicle Luxury Tax but it has not been properly published as a Gazette Notification. Later, the Petitioner got to know that the Draft Regulation on which he paid duty had not been properly published as a Gazette Notification on the day he paid duties. Hence, he moved to put an application before the Court of Appeal seeking reliefs of quashing the impugned Gazette Notification as well as refunding the paid duty as a Vehicle Luxury Tax for his imported vehicle. 

JUDGMENT:
** This Regulation has been published as a Gazette Notification under Section 51(1) of the Finance Act No 35 of 2018
** Since the Minister of Finance had made the Regulations within the purview of the provisions of the Finance Act, it did not mean the Regulation was ultra vires
** Respondents have acted within the scope of the provisions of the existing laws and regulations
** Petitioner failed to exhaust the alternative remedies provided in law - hence not entitled to seek writ jurisdictions
** Since the Petitioner has paid the Tax and get his vehicle released but took considerable time to institute this action - applies principle of estoppel by silence or acquiescence 
** Petitioner has not objected or protested or appeal against the imposition of the Tax when it was being cleared  - intentional act of surrender of his rights with knowledge
** Large numbers of vehicles have already been imposed with this Tax but non has come before Courts - hence the Gazette may not be quashed on the basis of disastrous consequences with regard to fiscal policy

HELD:
** A court before issuing a Writ of Mandamus, is entitled to take into consideration the consequences whic the issue of the writ will entail
** The Petitioner is not entitnled  to the reliefs as prayed
** Dismissed

    remarks => string (1026) "Issue: The Shipping and Forwarding Company of the Petitioner has issued Deli...
    $value[43]['remarks']
    Issue:
The Shipping and Forwarding Company of the Petitioner has issued Delivery Orders (DO) to a importing company which was later found does not exists, and as such, the Petitioner was imposed a penalty at the conclusion of the formal Customs Inquiry.
Petitioner contended that such imposing of penalty was illegal as he was not aware of the non-existence of the importing company, and hence, warrants issuance of Writs as granting of discretionary relief.

Judgement:
*The Petitioner has not contested the fact that the importing company is a fiction
*Conduct of the Petitioners does not warrants the issuance of the writs
*The Petitioner has to come with clean hands, and should candidly disclose all the material facts which have any bearing on the adjudication of the issues raised in the case
*He owes a duty of utmost good faith (uberrima fides) - full and complete disclosure of all material facts 

Held:
*He should have know by exercising diligence expected of a person of ordinary prudence.
*dismiss without cost

    remarks => string (1263) "***** The File can not be uploaded due to its size**** Issue: The Minister o...
    $value[45]['remarks']
    ***** The File can not be uploaded due to its size****
Issue:
The Minister of Finance has published an Extraordinary Gazette restricting importing of Turmeric, however, it has been delayed bringing it before the Parliament. The Petitioner has appealed that Gazette has no power as it has not been duly approved in Parliament.

Judgment:
* Section 20 of the Export & Import Control Act No 01 of 1969 provides for bringing the Regulation to the Parliament, is a directive
* The Minister is directed to bring the Regulation to the Parliament and he has no control over the Parliament then after
* In this Case, the Parliament has approved the Regulation by the time of impugned goods arrived and commercial invoice has been issued
* Thus, gazetted Regulation is legal and valid
* This importation triggers the Customs Ordinance and the controller General has no jurisdiction over the matter

Held:
* Importation without complying the provisions, triggers the Customs Ordinance, ousting the Jurisdiction of the Controller General in respect of the goods
* Controller of Import and Export is under no legal duty to issue a license to the Petitioner of this Case
* The Petitioner has  no legal rights to seek license if violates the Regulations
* Dismiss, without Costs

    ⇄⧉
    remarks => UTF-8 string (974) "The issue whether Sections 12, 44, 57, 59, 107 & 130 of the Customs Ordinanc...
    $value[48]['remarks']
    The issue
whether Sections 12, 44, 57, 59, 107 & 130 of the Customs Ordinance covers other goods stored in the same container with the contraband

The Judgment 
•	Section 107 is irrelevant to the issue as it was not considered in the Inquiring Order
•	The word “entered” in Section 57 of CO means “included in the Bill of Entry”
•	Section 57 does not warrant imposition of a forfeiture amounting to treble the value of goods
•	Section 59 allows imposition of treble the value of the goods as a forfeiture for only on “such goods”
•	The words “such goods” does not include all other goods entered or packed with the contraband
•	“Value of the goods” as mentioned in Section 59 means only the goods which Customs violation took place and not the goods entered or packed with the goods in relation of the violation.

Held
The Inquiring Officer’s decision is ultra vires 
Part of the Inquiring Order is quashed by issuing a Writ of Certiorari

    remarks => string (830) "Issue: There is a previous judgment into same matter under CA (Writ) 18/2019...
    $value[50]['remarks']
    Issue:
There is a previous judgment into same matter under CA (Writ) 18/2019 holding that the Minister of Finance has power under Special Commodity Levy Act No 48 of 2007 to rescind Gazette Orders by subsequent Orders. The Petitioner has appealed again to quash the Minister's Order and to compel Customs to accept the former Gazette Order for the particular shipment of Sugar.

Judgment:
* Article 128(1) and 128(2) of the Constitution has given rights to appeal
* The Minister has power under Section 2(3) of the Special Commodity Levy Act No 48 of 2007 to rescind Gazette Order
* In CA (Writ) 18/2019, it has given judgment that Ministers action is legal
* Appealing again without providing fresh facts has no merit to invoke the Judicature

Held:
No legal basis for the Application
Refuses to issue Notices
Dismissed with Cost

    title => string (93) "CA (Writ) 18/2019 - Ensen Trading & Industry Pvt Ltd Vs. Minister of Finance...

    ⇄url => string (40) "1622201401CA.WRIT.18.2019_correct_-_.pdf"
    $value[51]['url']
    ⇄date_modified => string (19) "2021-05-28 17:00:58"
    $value[51]['date_modified']
    ⇄⧉
    remarks => string (964) "Issue : Minister of Finance. by issuing a fresh Gazette Order has rescinded ...
    $value[51]['remarks']
    Issue :
Minister of Finance. by issuing a fresh Gazette Order has rescinded the previous Gazette Order which had included "Sugar" as a commodity coming under Special Commodity Levy Act No 48 of 2007.  The new Gazette has categorically included sugar as a commodity coming purview of Section 10 of the Customs Ordinance. The Petitioner has appealed stating that Minister's later Order has no legality as it has not been passed by the Parliament.

Judgment:
**The Article 148 of the Constitution has given the Parliament full control over Public Finance. 
**Rescinding a previous Gazette Order has not necessarily brought  impact on Public Finance. 
**If such Order is disapproved by the Parliament, it remains valid from the date of published until the refusal.
**Section 2(3) of the SCL Act has given power minister to amend, vary, rescind and revoke Orders

Held:
No legal basis for the claim and refuses to issue Notice, the Application dismissed without Costs

Minister of Finance. by issuing a fresh Gazette Order has rescinded the previous Gazette Order which had included "Sugar" as a commodity coming under Special Commodity Levy Act No 48 of 2007.  The new Gazette has categorically included sugar as a commodity coming purview of Section 10 of the Customs Ordinance. The Petitioner has appealed stating that Minister's later Order has no legality as it has not been passed by the Parliament.

Judgment:
**The Article 148 of the Constitution has given the Parliament full control over Public Finance. 
**Rescinding a previous Gazette Order has not necessarily brought  impact on Public Finance. 
**If such Order is disapproved by the Parliament, it remains valid from the date of published until the refusal.
**Section 2(3) of the SCL Act has given power minister to amend, vary, rescind and revoke Orders

Held:
No legal basis for the claim and refuses to issue Notice, the Application dismissed without Costs
    ⇄⧉

    ⇄⧉
    title => string (200) "SC (FR) APPLICATION 137/2016 - Thennakoon & Six Others Vs Hon. Attorney Gene...
    $value[53]['title']
    SC (FR) APPLICATION 137/2016 - Thennakoon & Six Others Vs Hon. Attorney General & Five Others - Articel 12(1) - Excise Duty - Letter of Credit - Budget Proposal - Nissan Electric Car  - Granted Relief
    ⇄url => string (75) "1623929131SC.FR.137.2018_-TENNAKOON___OTHERS_Vs_AG_AND_SEVEN__7__OTHERS.pdf"
    $value[53]['url']
    ⇄date_modified => string (19) "2021-06-17 16:54:05"
    $value[53]['date_modified']
    ⇄⧉
    remarks => string (1085) "Issue: Previously the Excise Duty for imported Nissan Electric Car was 5%, a...
    $value[53]['remarks']
    Issue:
Previously the Excise Duty for imported Nissan Electric Car was 5%, after the approval of the Budget it has been increased upto 50%, the Petitioners claimed that they were eligible for the former rates as the Letters of Credit had been opened prior to the Budget. The Ministry of Finance., later, issued Directions to allow such appeals subject to proofs that those LC had been lodged on behalf of the owner and the vehicle for personal use only. The Ministry has rejected few appeals on the basis of that those provided document did not conform with the requirements as prescribed by the Ministry.

Judgment:
The documents, provided by the Petitioners to prove that the imported vehicle for their personal, conforms with the directions as prescribed by the Ministry of Finance
Some monitory transactions were also admissible to prove that Petitioners (owners of the vehicles) had paid some money to the importer to reserve the vehicle and open LC on behalf of them

Held:
Respondents have violated Article 12(1) of the Constitution
Granted the reliefs sought by the Petitioners

    title => string (144) "CA (Writ) 84/2018 - Ruhunu Lanka Cement (Pvt) Ltd Vs. DGC - Transaction Valu...
    $value[54]['title']
    CA (Writ) 84/2018 - Ruhunu Lanka Cement (Pvt) Ltd Vs. DGC - Transaction Value  - Section 51A(2) - 52 - Shedule E - Article 8(1)(e)(ii) - Dismiss
    ⇄url => string (76) "1623157799CA.WRIT.84.2018_-_RUHUNU_LANKA_CEMENT_PVT_LTD_Vs_P_S_M_CHARLES.pdf"
    $value[54]['url']
    ⇄date_modified => string (19) "2021-06-08 18:36:11"
    $value[54]['date_modified']
    ⇄⧉
    remarks => string (928) "Issue: Petitioner seeks jurisdiction over declared value was correct, wherea...
    $value[54]['remarks']
    Issue:
Petitioner seeks jurisdiction over declared value was correct, whereas Customs contends that some additional cost incurred on handling of containers had been deliberately suppressed by the Petitioner

Judgment:
* The additional cost incurred on handling of containers up to the Port of Colombo
* That additional cost forms part of the Transaction Value
* According to WTO Agreement on Implementation of Article VII of the GATT 1994 - Transaction Value is accepted
* Section 51A invites declaration of values and Sec 51A(1) to furnish evidence to prove the declared values
* Article 8(1)(e)(ii)  allows SL Customs to adjust the value accounting charges on containers which was not included
* Applying of Section 52 by the Inquiring Officer to declare the forfeiture accords with the Law

Held:
Petitioner's intention to deliberately suppress the actual transaction value has been proven by SL Customs
The case is dismissed

    title => string (200) "CA (Writ) 82/2007 - Utsch Lanka Pvt Ltd Vs. Director General of Customs - Cl...
    $value[150]['title']
    CA (Writ) 82/2007 - Utsch Lanka Pvt Ltd Vs. Director General of Customs - Classification - Misdescription - Under valuation - Royalty - WCO - Sec 10, 47, 52 - Article 8(1)(c) of Schedule E - reliefs g
    ⇄url => string (41) "1659351163Utsch_Lanka_Pvt_Ltd_Vs._DGC.pdf"
    $value[150]['url']
    ⇄date_modified => string (19) "2016-12-27 17:35:24"
    $value[150]['date_modified']
    ⇄⧉
    remarks => string (1914) "Issue: The Petitioner had been importing aluminium plates which were intende...
    $value[150]['remarks']
    Issue:
The Petitioner had been importing aluminium plates which were intended to be printed and used as motor vehicle number plates. The Petitioner had relied on an advise sought and obtained by another company, and thereby the declared HS Code for the subsequent imports was 7616.00. After few years, the declared HS for the goods of the Petitioner was questioned by Customs and as a results the same was referred to the Classification and obtained HS code 8310.00 as the correct HS Code. Further, the Petitioner was charged not declaring Royalty in the Value Declaration Form. The inquiry  proceeded on the basis of suspicion that the offences of misdescription and undervaluation of the goods imported were committed.

Judgement:
*The Petitioner is bound to rely on the advice given by the Customs eventhough the Petitioner or its supplier holds a different opinion in relation to HS Code.
*Customs itself had doubts as to whether the number plates containing security features should be classified in heading 8310 or 7616.
*The Petitioner had relied on the former advice of the customs that the plate with security features falls under HS code 7616
*It can not be said to have been done with the intention of defrauding the Revenue as held in Toyota Lanka Judgment, that Section 47 would not apply in the absence of stealth.
*The WCO ruling had suggested the correct HS Code was 8310, hence only short levies was recoverable under Section 18 of the CO
*The Royalty that the Petitioner had come with the agreement with the mother company was for the operations (embossing and printing numbers) done after the importation of plates
*Therefore, the Royalty did not fall in the meaning of transaction value as mentioned in Article 8(1)(c) of Schedule E of the CO.

Held:
*No undervaluation by non declaration of royalty and no offences of misdescription
*issue a writ of certiorari to quash the inquiry order and
    ⇄⧉

    title => string (188) "CA (Writ) 1200/2002 - Rajapakse Vs. Director General of Customs - Motor Vehi...
    $value[308]['title']
    CA (Writ) 1200/2002 - Rajapakse Vs. Director General of Customs - Motor Vehicle - Chassis Number Tampered - Wilful use - Forged - Falsified - Counterfeit - Documents  - Sec 119 - Dismissed
    ⇄url => string (75) "1662715264CA__Writ__12002002_-_Rajapakse_Vs_Director_General_of_Customs.pdf"
    $value[308]['url']
    ⇄date_modified => string (19) "2016-12-28 13:04:44"
    $value[308]['date_modified']
    ⇄⧉
    remarks => string (1294) "ISSUE: The Petitioner had imported a vehicle and subsequently it was detaine...
    $value[308]['remarks']
    ISSUE:
The Petitioner had imported a vehicle and subsequently it was detained by Customs upon a ground of suspicion and found the Chassis number of the vehicle had been tampered, at the conclusion of the formal Customs inquiry, the Petitioner was acquitted as no knowledge was proved but declared forfeit the vehicles by the inquiry officer. The Petitioner challenged that the inquiry officer had acquitted him due to absence of knowledge the as to how declare forfeit goods under Section 119 as it required to establish "Wilful use when counterfeited or falsified any documents".

JUDGMENT:
* Petitioner had claimed that he had had no knowledge of fraud and not have wilfully used the same, and the legislature wanted to punish a person had an active role in defrauding but not merely use documents
* The Judge moved to explain as to how "wilful use" could be dealt in
* Tennakoon v DGC - importer used original certificates for purpose of clearance of the  vehicle is an admitted fact
                                  -  when it is used for the purpose of clearance, the use is wilful and such is established in law

HELD:
* in this instance case, false documents were submitted by the Petitioner for "wilful use of those documents" was established 
* Order is lawful
* Dismiss without cost
    ⇄⧉

    $value[333]['title']
    CA (Writ) 1386/2000 - Kuruwita Manchester Textiles Mills Ltd Vs. DGC - Valuation - Disposing Locally - Inquiry - Not Issuing Statements - Company as a Legal Person- Dismiss
    ⇄⧉
    url => string (111) "1623752180029-SLLR-SLLR-2003-V-3-KURUWITA-MANCHESTER-TEXTILE-MILLS-LTD-AND-A...
    $value[333]['url']
    1623752180029-SLLR-SLLR-2003-V-3-KURUWITA-MANCHESTER-TEXTILE-MILLS-LTD-AND-ANOTHER-v.-DIRECTOR-GENERAL-OF-C.pdf
    ⇄date_modified => string (19) "2021-06-15 15:44:33"

    ⇄⧉
    remarks => string (1431) "Issue: The Petitioner was allowed to disposing of un-exportable goods at loc...
    $value[333]['remarks']
    Issue:
The Petitioner was allowed to disposing of un-exportable goods at local market after paying due Customs Duty as stipulated in the BOI Agreement. It was disputed whether to consider Market Value or calculated Import Value. Petitioner further contended that non issuance of recorded Statements had caused violation of Natural Justice.

Judgment:
* Section 51 & 52 sets out to consider import value, Schedule E empowers Customs to determine values
* Section 8(1) has not given statutory provisions mandating Customs to issue statements 
* Customs Inquiry is a an administrative inquiry not a judicial inquiry
* In this case any how the Petitioner has been given opportunity to have the materials had used at the Inquiry
* Hence, no Natural Justice had been violated by the Customs
* Company has separate Legal entity distinct from the members and share holders
* General Manager has acted a representative of the Company
* Company has no physical existence in its affairs but managed by its agents
* Therefore, framing charges against the General Manager is valid

Held:
For the purpose of valuation, the import value + input values should be accounted, Customs has not been mandated to issue statement provided fair opportunities should be given to access for materials led at the Inquiry Company itself has legal identity whereas  Company has no physical existence in its affairs but managed by its agents.
Dismiss the Case
